title: Interpret dot plots with fraction operations
description: Interpret fraction data on dot plots to solve word problems.
topic_path: Interpret dot plots with fraction operations

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of paint left in $4$ cans. Each dot represents the volume of one can.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If we split the total amount of paint so that each can had the same amount, which image shows how much paint would be in each can?**        

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pints

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To split the paint equally, we need to take the extra from the cans that have more and give it to the cans that have less.

Some cans have as little as $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ pints, and some have as much as $5\dfrac{1}{2}$ pints. So if we split all the paint equally, each can would get an amount in between those.

Here are how full the cans are now, in pints ($\text{pt}$).

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's move $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ pints of paint from the fullest can to the least full can.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now each can has $4$ pints of paint.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

If we split the paint equally, each can would have ${4}$ pints of paint.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A line plot labeled Volume in pints from 2 to 6 with ticks every 1-half pint. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above two and one-half; 2 dots above four; and 1 dot above five and one-half.

choices.content: ![4 cans, each with 4 pints of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ebad321568f262ab8985144dc0f0d83199917164)
![4 cans, each with 3 pints of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c13ed9e1f2d08c1a25922d785f7d332ad7d318e4)
![4 cans, each with 4 and 1-half pints of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/406ab1816366384752c496230e8a44a7c12473cc)

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the amount of trail mix in the bags of $4$ different campers.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the campers shared the trail mix equally, which image shows how many ounces of trail mix would be in each bag?**
  
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To share the trail mix equally, we need to take the extra from the bags that have more and give it to the bags that have less.

Some bags had as little as $2\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces of trail mix, and some had as much as $3\dfrac{2}{4}$ ounces. So if the campers shared the trail mix equally, each bag would get an amount in between those.

Here are how full the bags are now, in ounces ($\text{oz}$).

[[☃ image 2]]

Let's move $\dfrac{2}{4}$ ounces of trail mix from the fullest bag to the least full bag.

[[☃ image 1]]

That got the bags closer to equal.

Now let's move $\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounce from the third bag to the first bag. Then we'll move $\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounce from the third bag to the second bag.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now each bag has $3$ ounces of trail mix.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

If the campers shared the trail mix equally, there would be $3$ ounces of trail mix in each bag.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt:
A line plot labeled mass of trail mix in ounces
from 2 to 4 with ticks every 1-fourth ounce. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above two and 1-fourth; 1 dot above two and 3-fourths; and 2 dots above three and one-half.

choices.content: ![4 bags, each with 2 and 2-fourths ounces of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/afbfb6b25bf6339cebec3c78cf98c222deeda6b8)
![4 bags, each with 2 and 3-fourths ounces of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/579b58adcbf5b681cff9bf9edde1d0fd810ae246)
![4 bags, each with 3 ounces of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/82d10219356a058888be6e7706ef8042607090ba)

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of juice in $5$ containers.

[[☃ image 1]]

Iver took the juice from the $2$ fullest containers. He divided the juice evenly into $3$ new containers.

**Which image shows how much juice is in each new container?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The fullest $2$ containers each had $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ liters. To split the juice equally, we need to take the extra from the containers that have more and give it to the empty container.

[[☃ image 1]]

If we split that equally into $3$ containers, then each container will have less than $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ liters.

Here are how full the containers are now, in liters ($\text{L}$).

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's move $2$ liters of juice from the first container to the empty container.

[[☃ image 1]]

That got the containers closer to equal.

Now let's move $1$ liter from the second container to the third container. Then we'll move $\dfrac{1}{2}$ liter from the second container to the first container.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now each container has $3$ liters of juice.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

There are ${3}$ liters of juice in each new container.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt:
A line plot labeled volumes of juice in liters
from 3 to 5 with ticks every 1-fourth liter. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above three and one-fourth and 2 dots above four and one-half.

choices.content: ![3 containers, each with 3 liters of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/51a7dfbe1fae073086696fe2f5754a0d05bd48a7)
![3 containers, each with 4 and 1-half liters of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3fd18538a9db21eaabac769d6278fe6c9f23adeb)
![3 containers, each with 3 and 3-fourths liters of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8affacb43ae24feecfa2431264d3b1d5f345949e)

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of sand used in $3$ sand castles. Each dot represents the volume of one sand castle.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If we split the total amount of sand so that each sand castle had the same amount, which image shows how much sand would be in each sand castle?**        

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] gallons

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To split the sand equally, we need to take the extra from the sand castles that have more and give it to the sand castles that have less.

Some sand castles have as little as $4\dfrac{1}{4}$ gallons, and some have as much as $5\dfrac{3}{4}$ gallons. So if we split all the sand equally, each sand castle would get an amount in between those.

Here are how full the sand castles are now, in gallons ($\text{pt}$).

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's move $\dfrac{3}{4}$ gallons of sand from the biggest sand castle to the smallest sand castle.

[[☃ image 1]]

That got the castles closer to equal.

Now let's move $\dfrac{1}{4}$ gallons from the second castle to the first castle. Then we'll move $\dfrac{1}{4}$ gallons from the second castle to the third castle.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now each sand castle has $5\dfrac{1}{4}$ gallons of sand.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

If we split the sand equally, each sand castle would have $5\dfrac{1}{4}$ gallons of sand.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A line plot labeled Volumes of sand, in gallons from 4 to 6 with ticks every 1-fourth gallon. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 4 and 1-fourth; and 2 dots above 5 and 3-fourths.

choices.content: ![3 buckets, each with 5 and 1-fourth gallons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c3b300d605f19e0b0cd0b17554c3759c8bd2ccc5)
![3 buckets, each with 5 gallons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ac252d86ecbca0b604a818545a381418724bc87a)
![3 buckets, each with 5 and 3-fourths gallons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d18967e055fc87197c7f1d7a41449a44eee08932)

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of glitter in $6$ tubes.

[[☃ image 1]]

Cameron took the glitter from the $3$ least full tubes. They divided the glitter evenly into $2$ new tubes.

**Which image shows how much glitter is in each new tube?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The $3$ least full tubes each had $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ tablespoons. To split the glitter equally, we need to take all the glitter from the last tube and split it between the other $2$ tubes.

[[☃ image 1]]

If we split that equally into $2$ tubes, then each tube will have more than $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ tablespoons.

Here are how full the tubes are now, in tablespoons ($\text{Tbsp}$).

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's move $\dfrac{1}{2}$ tablespoon of glitter from the third tube to each of the other tubes.

[[☃ image 1]]

That got the third tube closer to empty.

There's $\dfrac{1}{2}$ tablespoon left in the third tube, which equals $\dfrac{2}{4}$ tablespoons. Now let's split it and move $\dfrac{1}{4}$ tablespoon from the third tube to each of the other tubes.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now the glitter is in $2$ tubes with $2\dfrac{1}{4}$ tablespoon of glitter each.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

There are $2\dfrac{1}{4}$ tablespoons of glitter in each new tube.

[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt:
A line plot labeled volumes of glitter, in tablespoons,
from 1 to 3 with ticks every 1-fourth tablespoon. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above 1 and 1-half; 2 dots above 2 and 1-half; and 1 dot above 3.

choices.content: ![2 tubes, each with 2 and 1-fourth tablespoons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eb0a83b737b8ab547992e99ec142c16325d43ac1)
![2 tubes, each with 2 and 3-fourths tablespoons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ad32a0fd09a9347a9b317f817461e59c9a6144f)
![2 tubes, each with 3 tablespoons of contents.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a6ccb6b08a537f21474dd5efe06e3b85a3b9ea8b)

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of $11$ river water samples collected by an ecologist.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many times as great is the volume of the largest sample than the volume of the smallest sample?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] times as great
hints:
The smallest sample was $\blueE{\dfrac18}$ gallon of water.


[[☃ image 1]]

The largest sample was $\maroonD{\dfrac78}$ gallon of water.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{8}}\times\tealE{\,?}=\maroonE{\dfrac78}$
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{8}}\times\tealE{7}=\maroonE{\dfrac78}$
The volume of the largest sample is $\tealE7$ times as great than the volume of the smallest sample.

options.alt: A line plot labeled volume of water, in gallons, from 0 to 1 with ticks every 1-eighth gallon. Dots are plotted as follows: 2 dots above one-eighth; 1 dot above one-fourth; 1 dot above three-eighths; 3 dots above five-eighths; 2 dots above three-fourths; and 2 dots above seven-eighths.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the mass of each of $9$ puppies in a litter.


[[☃ image 1]]

**How many times as great is the mass of the heaviest puppy than the mass of the lightest puppy?**  
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac18$ pound.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  times as great
hints:
The lightest puppy weighed $\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ of a pound.


[[☃ image 1]]

The heaviest puppy weighed $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ of a pound.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\times\purpleD{\,?}=\maroonE{\dfrac34}$
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\times\purpleD{3}=\maroonE{\dfrac34}$
The heaviest puppy's mass is $\purpleE{3}$ times as great as the lightest puppy's mass.

options.alt:
A line plot labeled mass of puppies in pounds
from 0 to 1 with ticks every 1-eighth pound. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above one-fourth; 1 dot above one-half; 4 dots above five-eighths,  and 3 dots above three-fourths.

assessment_items.question_content:
During a winter storm, the amount of rainfall was recorded in $5$ different locations.

[[☃ image 1]]

A $6^{\text{th}}$ location (not on the plot) received $\dfrac{1}{2}$ as much rain as the location with the most rain. 

**How many inches of rain fell at the $6^{\text{th}}$ location?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints: To get $\dfrac{1}{2}$ as much rain means to get $\dfrac{1}{2}$ times as much. So we need to multiply $\dfrac{1}{2}$ times the largest amount of rain.

The location with the most rain received $3\dfrac{1}{2}$ inches of rain.

[[☃ image 1]]

The sixth location received $\dfrac{1}{2}$ as much rain.

$\begin{align}
\blueE{3\dfrac{1}{2}}\times \dfrac{1}{2} &= \left(\blueE{3}\times \dfrac{1}{2}\right) + \left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{2}}\times \dfrac{1}{2}\right)\\\\
&= \dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{1}{4}\\\\
&= \dfrac{6}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4}\\\\
&= \dfrac{7}{4}
\end{align}$

The sixth location received $\dfrac{7}{4}$ inches of rain.

The other measurements used mixed numbers. We can rewrite $\dfrac{7}{4}$ as $1\dfrac{3}{4}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This makes sense, because $3\dfrac{1}{2}$ is between $2$ and $4$. So $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $3\dfrac{1}{2}$ should be between $1$ and $2$.
The $6^{\text{th}}$ location received $1\dfrac{3}{4}$ inches of rain.

options.alt: A line plot labeled rainfall in inches from 0 to 4 with ticks every 1-half inch. Dots are plotted as follows: 2 dots above one-half; 1 dot above two; 1 dot above two and one-half; and 1 dot above three and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the weight of $9$ box turtles at the nature center.

[[☃ image 1]]

The nature center rescued a $10^{\text{th}}$ turtle (not on the plot). The turtle's weight was $\dfrac{3}{4}$ as much as the heaviest turtle. 

**What was the weight of the $10^{\text{th}}$ turtle?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds
hints: To weight $\dfrac{3}{4}$ as much means to weight $\dfrac{3}{4}$ times as much. So we need to multiply $\dfrac{3}{4}$ times the heaviest weight.

The heaviest turtle weighed $2$ pounds.

[[☃ image 1]]

The tenth turtle weighed $\dfrac{3}{4}$ as many pounds.

$\blueE{2}\times \dfrac{3}{4} = \dfrac{6}{4}$

The tenth turtle weighed $\dfrac{6}{4}$ pounds.

The other measurements used mixed numbers. We can rewrite $\dfrac{6}{4}$ as $1\dfrac{1}{2}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The $10^{\text{th}}$ turtle weighed $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ pounds.

options.alt: A line plot labeled weight of box turtles, in pounds, from 0 to 2 with ticks every 1-fourth pound. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 3-fourths; 2 dots above 3-fourths; 1 dot above 1-half; 3 dots above 3-fourths; and 2 dots above 2.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the amount of liquid remaining in $5$ basketball players' water bottles after practice.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many times as great is the amount of water remaining in the bottle with the most water than the bottle with the least water?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] times as great
hints:
The bottle with the least amount of water remaining has $\blueE{\dfrac14}$ pint.


[[☃ image 1]]

The bottle with the most water remaining has $\maroonD{\dfrac34}$ pint.



[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\times\purpleD{{?}}=\maroonD{\dfrac34}$
$\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}\times\purpleD{3}=\maroonD{\dfrac34}$
The amount of water remaining in the bottle with the most water is $\purpleD{3}$ times as great as the amount of water remaining in the bottle with the least water.

options.alt:
A line plot labeled volume of remaining liquid in pints
from 0 to 1 with ticks every 1-fourth pint. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above one-fourth, 3 dots above one-half; and 1 dot above three-fourths.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amarilis started with $20$ inches of string. She tried an experiment to lift ice with pieces of string of different lengths. The plot below shows the lengths of $3$ strings she used.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much unused string did Amarilis have left after the experiment?**   
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac14$ inch.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints:
First, we can add the lengths from the experiment to find out how much string Amarilis used. 

Then we can subtract the total from what she started with to see how much is left.

Amarilis used pieces of string that were ${3}$,  ${4\dfrac{1}{4}}$, and $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ inches long.

[[☃ image 2]]

Let's add those lengths.

$\begin{align}
3+\blueE{4\dfrac{1}{4}}+\maroonD{4\dfrac{1}{1}}&= 3+\blueE{4+\dfrac{1}{4}}+\maroonD{4+\dfrac{1}{1}}\\\\
&=(3+\blueE{4}+\maroonD{4}) + \left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)\\\\
&=11 +  \left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)
\end{align}$

Now we subtract to see how much string was left over.

$\purpleD{20}-{11\dfrac{3}{4}}$

There's no fraction part to $\purpleD{20}$, so let's regroup to $\purpleD{19\dfrac{4}{4}}$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{19}\purpleD{\dfrac{4}{4}}&\\\\
\underline{-{11}{\dfrac{3}{4}}}&\\
8\dfrac{1}{4}
\end{align}$
Amarilis has $8\dfrac{1}{4}$ inches of unused string left.

options.alt: A line plot labeled length of strings in inches from 3 to 5 with ticks every 1-fourth inch. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 3; 1 dot above 4 and 1-fourth; and 1 dot above 4 and 1-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the weight of $12$ pumpkins at Jack-O-Lantern farm.

[[☃ image 1]]

Clyde picked the lightest pumpkin. Koen picked the two heaviest pumpkins.

**How much more do Koen's $2$ pumpkins combined weigh than Clyde's pumpkin weighs?**   
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac14$ pound.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds
hints:
First, let's find the combined weight of Koen's $2$ pumpkins.

Then we can find the difference between the total weight of Koen's pumpkins and Clyde's pumpkin by subtracting.

The heaviest two pumpkins each weighed $\blueE{14\dfrac{1}{2}}$ pounds.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueE{14\dfrac{1}{2}}+\blueE{14\dfrac{1}{2}}&=14+14+ \dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{1}{2}\\\\
&=28+\dfrac{2}{2}\\\\
&=28+1\\\\
&=\purpleD{29}\text{ lbs}
\end{align}$

Koen's pumpkins weighed a total of $\purpleD{29}\,\text{lbs}$.

Clyde's pumpkin, the lightest pumpkin, weighed $\maroonD{13\dfrac{3}{4}}$ pounds.

[[☃ image 1]]

Finally, we subtract the weights to find the difference.

$\blueE{29}-\maroonD{13\dfrac{3}{4}}$

There's no fraction part to $\blueE{29}$, so let's regroup to $\blueE{28\dfrac{4}{4}}$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
\blueE{28\dfrac{4}{4}}\\
\underline{-\maroonD{13\dfrac{3}{4}}}\\
\goldE{15\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$
Koen's pumpkins combined weigh $\goldE{15\dfrac{1}{4}}$ pounds more than Clyde's pumpkin weighs.

options.alt: A line plot labeled weight of pumpkins (pounds) from 13 to 15 with ticks every 1-fourth pound. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 13 and 3-fourths; 5 dots above 14; 4 dots above 14 and 1-fourth; 2 dots above 14 and 1-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of juice squeezed from $20$ oranges.


[[☃ image 1]]

Nicole mixed the juice from the $2$ oranges with the least juice into yogurt. Nicole drank the juice from the orange with the most juice.

**How much more orange juice did Nicole drink than she mixed into her yogurt?**   
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac12$ fluid ounce.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fluid ounces
hints:
The two oranges with the least juice had $\blueE{1}$ fluid ounce and $\blueE{1\dfrac{1}{2}}$ fluid ounces of juice.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{1}+\blueE{1\dfrac{1}{2}}=\purpleD{2\dfrac{1}{2}}$

Nicole mixed $\purpleD{2\dfrac{1}{2}}$ fluid ounces of juice into her yogurt.

The orange with the most juice had $\maroonD{5\dfrac{1}{2}}$ fluid ounces of juice.


[[☃ image 1]]

Nicole drank $\maroonD{5\dfrac{1}{2}}$ fluid ounces of juice.

Now, we subtract to see how much more juice Nicole drank than she mixed into her yogurt.

$\purpleD{5\dfrac12}-\maroonD{2\dfrac12}=\goldE{3}$
Nicole drank $\goldE{3}$ more fluid ounces of juice than she mixed into her yogurt.

options.alt:
A line plot labeled volume of juice in fluid ounces
from 0 to 6 with ticks every 1-half fluid ounce. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above one; 1 dot above one and one-half; 5 dots above two and one-half; 6 dots above three; 4 dots above three and one-half; 2 dots above four and one-half; 1 dot above five and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the lengths of $5$ neck warmers that Mary knit for a family of giraffes. 


[[☃ image 1]]

The shortest warmer was blue. The two longest warmers were pink.

**How much longer was the combined length of the pink warmers than the length of the blue warmer?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] feet
hints:
The two longest warmers were  $\maroonD{6 \dfrac12}$ feet and $\purpleD{6}$ feet long.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can find the total length of the pink warmers by adding.

 $\maroonD{6 \dfrac12}+\purpleD{6} = \maroonD{6} + \purpleD{6} + \maroonD{\dfrac12} = 12\dfrac12$

The shortest warmer was $\blueE{4\dfrac{1}{2}}$ feet long.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can subtract to find the difference between the total length of the pink warmers and the length of the blue warmer.

$12\dfrac12-\blueE{4\dfrac12} = 8$
The combined length of the pink warmers was $8$ feet longer than than the length of the blue warmer.

options.alt: A line plot labeled length of neck warmer in feet from 4 to 7 with ticks every 1-half foot. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above four and one-half; 2 dots above five; 1 dot above six and 1 dot above six and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of vinegar used by each of $16$ students in their volcano experiments.

[[☃ image 1]]

The $3$ students who used the most vinegar were in a group. They were supposed to use a total of $9$ fluid ounces of vinegar.

**How much extra vinegar did the $3$ students in the group use?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fluid ounces
hints:
The students in the group all used the same amount of vinegar. We can multiply the amount by $3$ to find the total amount they used.

Then we can subtract the amount of vinegar they were supposed to use to find the difference.

The $3$ largest samples all have a volume of $3\dfrac12$ fluid ounces.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply to find the total volume.

$\begin{align}\text{total}&=\blueE{3\dfrac12}\times{3}\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{3+\dfrac12}\right)\times 3\\\\
&=(3\times3)+\left(\dfrac12\times{3}\right)\\\\
&=9 + \dfrac{3}{2}
\end{align}$

Since $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{2}}=\maroonD{1\dfrac{1}{2}}$, we can add for the total volume.

$\begin{align}9+\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{2}}&=9+\maroonD{1\dfrac{1}{2}}\\\\
&=10\dfrac{1}{2}
\end{align}$

The group used a total of $10\dfrac{1}{2}$ fluid ounces of vinegar.

They were only supposed to use $9$ fluid ounces.

$10\dfrac{1}{2}-9=1\dfrac{1}{2}$
The group used $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ extra fluid ounces of vinegar.

options.alt: A line plot labeled volume of vinegar in fluid ounces from 0 to 4 with ticks every 1-half fluid ounce. Dots are plotted as follows: 4 dots above one-half; 3 dots above one; 5 dots above two and one-half; 1 dot above three; and 3 dots above three and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The lengths of $4$ of Avi's favorite songs are recorded below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total length of all of Avi's favorite songs?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints: To find the total length of all of Avi's favorite songs, we need to add all of the song lengths.

One song is $\blueE{4\dfrac{1}{4}}$ and another song is $\blueE{4\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The longest two songs are each $\maroonD{5\dfrac{1}{2}}$ minutes long.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can add to find the total length of the songs.

$\begin{align}\text{Total time}&=\blueE{4\dfrac{1}{4}} + \blueE{4\dfrac{3}{4}} + \maroonD{5\dfrac{1}{2}}+ \maroonD{5\dfrac{1}{2}}\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{4+\dfrac{1}{4}}\right) + \left(\blueE{4+\dfrac{3}{4}} \right)+\left(\maroonD{5+\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)+\left(\maroonD{5+\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)\\\\
&=(\blueE{4}+ \blueE{4} + \maroonD{5}+\maroonD{5})+\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{4}} +\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)\\\\
&=18+2\\\\
&=20
\end{align}$
The total length of all of Avi's favorite songs is $20$ minutes.

options.alt: A line plot labeled length of songs in minutes from 4 to 6 with ticks every 1-fourth minute. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above four and one-fourth; 1 dot above four and three-fourths; and 2 dots above five and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The weight of $4$ hummingbirds is recorded below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the weight of all $4$ hummingbirds combined?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] grams
hints: To find the total weight of all of the hummingbirds, we need to add their weights.

The birds weigh $2\dfrac{1}{2}$ grams, $3$ grams, $4\dfrac{1}{2}$ grams, and $5$ grams.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can add to find the total weight of all $4$ hummingbirds combined.

$\begin{align}\text{Total weight}&=2\dfrac12+3 +4\dfrac12+{5}\\\\
&=2+3+4+ 5 +\dfrac12+\dfrac12\\\\
&=14+\dfrac22\\\\
&=14+1\\\\
&=15
\end{align}$
The weight of all $4$ hummingbirds combined is $15$ grams.

options.alt: A line plot labeled weights of birds in grams from 2 to 5 with ticks every 1-half gram. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above two and one-half; 1 dot above three; 1 dot above four and one-half, and 1 dot above five.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the amount of time it takes $4$ students to run a mile.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total time of the $4$ students?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints: To find the total time, we can add each student's time.

The running times of the students are $7\dfrac{1}{4}$ minutes, $7\dfrac{3}{4}$ minutes, $8\dfrac{1}{2}$ minutes, and $8\dfrac{1}{2}$ minutes.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can add to find the total time.

$\begin{align}\text{Total time}&=\purpleD{7\dfrac14}+\maroonD{7\frac34}+\blueE{8\frac12}+\blueE{8\frac12}\\\\
&=(\purpleD7+\maroonD{7}+\blueD8+\blueD8)+\left(\purpleD{\dfrac14}+\maroonD{\dfrac34}\right)+\left(\blueE{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\blueE{\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)\\\\
&=30+\dfrac{4}{4}+\dfrac{2}{2}\\\\
&=30+1+1\\\\
&=\tealE{32}
\end{align}$
The students had a total time of $\tealE{32}$ minutes.

options.alt: A line plot labeled running times in minutes from 7 to 9 with ticks every 1-fourth minute. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above 7 and 1-fourth, 1 dot above 7 and 3-fourths, and 2 dots above eight and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the volume of each of $5$ creek water samples taken by a biologist.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total volume of water in the $5$ samples?**  
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac14$ cup.*
  
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cups
hints:
To find the total volume, we can add the volumes of each sample. 

Since there are repeated volumes, we can multiply instead of adding those over and over.

There are $3$ samples with a volume of $5\dfrac{1}{4}$ each. There are $2$ samples with a volume of $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ each.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply and add to find the total volume.

$\begin{align}\text{Total volume}&=\blueE{5\dfrac{1}{4}}\times 3 + \maroonE{6\dfrac{1}{2}}\times 2\\\\
&= \left(\blueE{5+\dfrac{1}{4}}\right)\times 3 + \left(\maroonE{6+\dfrac{1}{2}}\right)\times 2\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{5}\times 3\right)+\left(\blue{\dfrac{1}{4}} \times 3\right) + \left(\maroonE{6}\times 2\right)+\left(\maroonE{\dfrac{1}{2}}\times 2\right)\\\\
&=15+\dfrac{3}{4}+12+\dfrac{2}{2}
\end{align}$
The total volume of the $5$ samples is $28\dfrac{3}{4}$ cups of water.

options.alt: A line plot labeled volume of creek water samples in cups from 5 to 7 with ticks every 1-fourth cup. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above five and one-fourth; and 2 dots above six and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the amount of rainfall in  $6$ counties during the month of July.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total number of inches of rain that fell?**   
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac14$ inch.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches
hints:
To find the total rainfall, we can add the depth of rain from each county. 

Since there are repeated depths, we can multiply instead of adding those over and over.

There are $3$ counties with $\dfrac{3}{4}$ inches of rainfall each. There are $3$ counties with $1\dfrac{1}{4}$ inches of rainfall each.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply and add to find the total amount of rainfall.

$\begin{align}\text{Total inches}&=\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\times 3 + \blueE{1\dfrac{1}{4}}\times{3}\\\\
&=\left(\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}\times 3\right) + \left(\blueE{1+\dfrac{1}{4}}\right)\times3\\\\
&=\dfrac{9}{4} + (1\times3)+\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\times{3}\right)\\\\
&=\dfrac{9}{4} +3 + \dfrac{3}{4}\\\\
&= 3 + \dfrac{12}{4}
\end{align}$

Since $\goldE{\dfrac{12}{4}}$ equals $\goldE{3}$, we can add for the total volume.

$\begin{align}
3 + \goldE{\dfrac{12}{4}} &= 3 + \goldE{3}\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$
A total of $6$ inches of rain fell.

options.alt: A line plot labeled Rainfall in Virginia counties in inches from 0 to 2 with ticks every 1-fourth inch. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above three-fourths, and 3 dots above one and one-fourth.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the mass of salt samples used by each of $10$ students in a science experiment.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total mass of the $5$ smallest salt samples?**    

  
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] ounces
hints:
The $5$ smallest samples all have a mass of $\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply to find the total mass.

$\begin{align}
\text{total}&=\frac{1}{4}\times{5}\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{4}\times\dfrac51\\\\
&=\frac{5}{4}\\\\
&=1\frac{1}{4}
\end{align}$
The total mass of the $5$ smallest samples is $1\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces.

options.alt: A line plot labeled mass of salt samples (ounces) from 0 to 2 with ticks every 1-fourth ounce. Dots are plotted as follows: 5 dots above one-fourth; 2 dots above one-half; 1 dot above one, and 2 dots above one and three-fourths.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the length of each of the $7$ bulletin boards hanging in the fifth grade hallway.

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the $3$ longest bulletin boards were side by side, what would their total length be?**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
The longest bulletin board has a length of $\blueE{4}$ meters.


[[☃ image 1]]

The next longest bulletin boards have a length of $\maroonD{3\dfrac12}$ meters.

[[☃ image 1]]

We add the lengths to find the total distance.

$\begin{align}\text{Total distance}&=\maroonD{3\frac12}+\maroonD{3\frac12}+\blueE{4}&\\\\
&=\maroonD3+\maroonD3+\blueE4+\maroonD{\dfrac12}+\maroonD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&=10+\dfrac22\\\\
&=10+1\\\\
&=11
\end{align}$
If the $3$ longest bulletin boards were side by side, their total length would be $11$ meters.

options.alt: A line plot labeled length in meters from 1 to 4 with ticks every 1-half meter. Dots are plotted as follows: 1 dot above one; 1 dot above one and one-half; 2 dots above three; 2 dots above three and one-half and 1 dot above four.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the weight of each of $10$ female Spiny Dogfish sharks.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total weight of the $3$ lightest sharks?**  
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac18$ pound.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds
hints:
The $3$ lightest sharks all have a weight of $3\dfrac{3}{8}$ pounds.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply to find the total weight. 

$\begin{align}
\text{Total weight }&=\blueE{3\dfrac{3}{8}}\times{3}\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{3+\dfrac{3}{8}}\right)\times3\\\\
&=(3\times3)+\left(\dfrac{3}{8}\times{3}\right)\\\\
&=9 + \dfrac{9}{8}
\end{align}$

Since $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{8}}=\maroonD{1\dfrac{1}{8}}$, we can add for the total weight.

$\begin{align}9+\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{8}}&=9+\maroonD{1\dfrac{1}{8}}\\\\
&=10\dfrac{1}{8}
\end{align}$
The total weight of the $3$ lightest sharks is $10\dfrac{1}{8}$ pounds.

options.alt: A line plot labeled weight of sharks in pounds from 3 to 4 and 1-half with ticks every 1-eighth pound. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above three and three-eighths, 2 dots above four, 3 dots above four and one-eighth, and 2 dots above four and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The amount of time it took $5$ mice to navigate a maze is recorded below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many seconds all together did it take the $3$ mice with the shortest times to navigate the maze?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] seconds
hints:
It took the $3$ mice with the shortest times $\blueE{9 \dfrac34}$ seconds each to navigate the maze.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can find the total time for the $3$ mice that took the shortest amount of time by multiplying.

$\begin{align}\text{Total time}&=\blueE{9\dfrac{3}{4}}\times{3}\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{9+\dfrac{3}{4}}\right)\times3\\\\
&=(9\times3)+\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\times{3}\right)\\\\
&=27 + \dfrac{9}{4}
\end{align}$

Since $\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{4}}=\maroonD{2\dfrac{1}{4}}$, we can add this to $27$ to find the total time.

$\begin{align}27+\maroonD{\dfrac{9}{4}}&=27+\maroonD{2\dfrac{1}{4}}\\\\
&=\goldE{29\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$
It took the $3$ mice with the shortest times $\goldE{29 \dfrac14}$ seconds all togther to navigate the maze.

options.alt: A line plot labeled times to finish the maze (seconds) from 9 to 11 with ticks every 1-fourth second. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above nine and three-fourths; 1 dot above ten; and 1 dot above ten and one-half.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the weight of each of $15$ ostrich eggs.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the total weight of the $3$ heaviest eggs?**   
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac18$   pound.*
   
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds
hints:
The $3$ heaviest eggs all have a weight of $\blueE{1\dfrac{5}{8}}$ pounds.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply to find the total weight.

$\begin{align}\text{Total weight}&=\blueE{1\dfrac{5}{8}}\times{3}\\\\
&=\left(\blueE{1+\dfrac{5}{8}}\right)\times3\\\\
&=(1\times3)+\left(\dfrac{5}{8}\times{3}\right)\\\\
&=3 + \dfrac{15}{8}
\end{align}$

Since $\maroonD{\dfrac{15}{8}}=\maroonD{1\dfrac{7}{8}}$, we can add for the total weight.

$\begin{align}3+\maroonD{\dfrac{15}{8}}&=3+\maroonD{1\dfrac{7}{8}}\\\\
&=4\dfrac{7}{8}
\end{align}$
The total weight of the $3$ heaviest eggs is $4\dfrac{7}{8}$ pounds.

options.alt: A line plot labeled weight of eggs in pounds from 1 to 2 with ticks every 1-eighth pound. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above one; 4 dots above one and one eighth; 2 dots above one and three-eights; 3 dots above one and one-half; and 3 dots above one and five-eighths.

assessment_items.question_content:
The plot below shows the distance traveled by Bus $49$ between each of its $11$ stops.


[[☃ image 1]]

The $4$ shortest distances are for in-a-row stops.

**What is the total distance Bus $49$ would travel, if it only went the $4$ shortest distances?**    
*All measurements are rounded to the nearest $\dfrac12$ kilometer.*  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilometers
hints:
The $3$ shortest distances are each $\blueE{\dfrac12}$ of a kilometer.


[[☃ image 1]]

The fourth shortest distance is $\maroonD{1}$ kilometer.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\text{Total distance}&=\blueE{\dfrac12}+\blueE{\dfrac12}+\blueE{\dfrac12}+\maroonD{1}\\\\
&=\dfrac32+1\\\\
&=1\dfrac12+1\\\\
&=2\dfrac12
\end{align}$
The bus would travel $2\dfrac12$ kilometers.

options.alt: A line plot labeled distance bus 49 travels in kilometers from 0 to 4 with ticks every 1-half kilometer. Dots are plotted as follows: 3 dots above 1-half; 1 dot above 1; 4 dots above 1 and 1-half; 1 dot above 2, 1 dot above 3 and 1-half; and 1 dot above 4.
